title: Remove subtree queries and report tree height
description: For multiple queries where a subtree rooted at node x is removed, determine the height of the remaining tree after each query

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build tree with root 1, children: 2,3; node2 children: 4,5. Step 2: Query 1 - remove subtree at node2. Mark nodes 2,4,5 as removed. Remaining nodes: 1,3. Calculate height: BFS from root1, only node3 at depth1, height=1 (max depth). Actually height is longest path: node1 to node3 has depth1, so height=1. Wait, tree height is max depth from root, so height=1. Output 1. Step 3: Query 2 - remove subtree at node3. Mark node3 as removed. Remaining nodes: 1 only. Height=0 (only root). Output 0. Final outputs: [1,0]. Correction: After first query, remaining tree has nodes 1 and 3, path 1-3 length 1, height=1. After second query, only node1 remains, height=0. So output [1,0].

Step 1: Build tree: root1 children 2,3; node2 child4; node3 child5. Step 2: Query1 remove subtree at node3. Mark nodes 3,5 removed. Remaining: 1,2,4. Height: BFS from root1, nodes at depth1:2, depth2:4. Max depth=2, height=2. Output 2. Step 3: Query2 remove subtree at node2. Mark nodes 2,4 removed. Remaining: only node1. Height=0. Output 0. Final [2,0].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recalculation
approaches.summary: For each query, mark the subtree rooted at node x as removed, then recompute the height of the remaining tree by performing a full DFS/BFS traversal. This is straightforward but inefficient for multiple queries.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(q * n) where q is number of queries, n is number of nodes

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recalculation for Remove Subtree Queries
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the brute force approach to handle multiple subtree removal queries and report tree height after each removal.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the brute force recalculation approach. The intuition is straightforward: for each query, we mark the entire subtree rooted at the given node as removed, then recompute the height of the remaining tree from scratch. This works because after each removal, we treat the remaining nodes as a new tree and calculate its height using standard tree height algorithms. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Parse input to build the tree structure (adjacency list). 2) For each query node x: a) Mark all nodes in subtree rooted at x as removed using DFS. b) Perform BFS/DFS from the root (if not removed) to find the maximum depth among remaining nodes. c) Output this height. Time complexity is O(q * n) where q is queries and n is nodes, as each query may traverse all nodes twice (for marking and height calculation). Space is O(n) for adjacency list and removal markers. This connects to L1 by using the subtree marking function and height calculation as key subroutines. Common mistakes include: not handling root removal (height becomes 0), incorrect adjacency list building, or forgetting to reset removal markers between queries (not needed here since removals are cumulative). Real code pattern: Build adjacency list, maintain removed boolean array, for each query: DFS to mark subtree, BFS from root to find max depth among non-removed nodes.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build tree with root 1, children: 2,3; node2 children: 4,5. Step 2: Query 1 - remove subtree at node2. Mark nodes 2,4,5 as removed. Remaining nodes: 1,3. Calculate height: BFS from root1, only node3 at depth1, height=1 (max depth). Actually height is longest path: node1 to node3 has depth1, so height=1. Wait, tree height is max depth from root, so height=1. Output 1. Step 3: Query 2 - remove subtree at node3. Mark node3 as removed. Remaining nodes: 1 only. Height=0 (only root). Output 0. Final outputs: [1,0]. Correction: After first query, remaining tree has nodes 1 and 3, path 1-3 length 1, height=1. After second query, only node1 remains, height=0. So output [1,0].

Step 1: Build tree: root1 children 2,3; node2 child4; node3 child5. Step 2: Query1 remove subtree at node3. Mark nodes 3,5 removed. Remaining: 1,2,4. Height: BFS from root1, nodes at depth1:2, depth2:4. Max depth=2, height=2. Output 2. Step 3: Query2 remove subtree at node2. Mark nodes 2,4 removed. Remaining: only node1. Height=0. Output 0. Final [2,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Mark Subtree as Removed via DFS
ladders.desc: DFS traversal to mark all nodes in a subtree as removed, given a root node.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function: given a tree and a node x, mark all nodes in the subtree rooted at x as removed. The intuition is that removal of a subtree means all descendants of x should be excluded from future calculations. Using DFS allows us to recursively visit every node in the subtree efficiently. Algorithm: 1) Start at node x, mark it as removed. 2) For each child of x in the adjacency list, recursively call the function. 3) Base case: if node has no children (leaf), just mark it and return. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the size of the subtree, which could be O(n) in worst case. Space is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is height of subtree. This feeds into L0 by providing the removal marking step. Common mistakes: not marking the root node x itself, incorrect adjacency list traversal (e.g., treating undirected edges as directed), or infinite recursion in cycles (not an issue in trees). Real code pattern: def mark_subtree(node, removed, adj): removed[node]=True; for child in adj[node]: mark_subtree(child, removed, adj).

examples.input: Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,4],[2,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node2, mark removed. Step 2: Check children of 2 from adjacency list: nodes 4 and 5. Step 3: Recurse to node4, mark removed, no children, return. Step 4: Recurse to node5, mark removed, no children, return. Done. Removed nodes are 2,4,5.

examples.input: Tree edges: [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node3
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node3, mark removed. Step 2: Children of 3: node4. Step 3: Recurse to node4, mark removed, no children, return. Done. Removed nodes: 3,4.

testCases.input: Tree edges: [[1,2]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node1
Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node3
Tree edges: [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node2
Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,4],[2,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node1
Tree edges: [[1,2],[2,3],[1,4]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node4
Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3],[3,4],[4,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node3
Tree edges: [[1,2],[2,3],[2,4],[3,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node2
Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3],[1,4],[2,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node1
Tree edges: [[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[4,5]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node4
Tree edges: [[1,2],[1,3],[2,4],[3,5],[5,6]], Root: 1, Mark subtree at node3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Tree Height via BFS/DFS
ladders.desc: Calculate the height of a tree given its root and a set of removed nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is computing tree height while ignoring removed nodes. Height is defined as the maximum depth from the root to any leaf in the remaining tree. Intuition: we need to traverse the tree, tracking depth, and find the maximum depth among nodes that are not removed. BFS (level-order traversal) is natural because it processes nodes level by level, and the last level reached gives the height. Alternatively, DFS can compute max depth recursively. Algorithm using BFS: 1) If root is removed, return 0. 2) Initialize queue with (root, depth=0). 3) While queue not empty: pop node, if not removed, update max depth, push children with depth+1. 4) Return max depth. Time O(n) as we may visit all nodes. Space O(n) for queue. This feeds into L0 by providing the height calculation after removals. Common mistakes: counting edges vs nodes (height as edges: depth count; here we use depth count from root, so leaf at depth d gives height d), not skipping removed nodes in traversal, or incorrect adjacency handling. Real code pattern: def tree_height(root, removed, adj): if removed[root]: return 0; queue=[(root,0)]; max_depth=0; while queue: node,depth=queue.pop(0); if not removed[node]: max_depth=max(max_depth,depth); for child in adj[node]: queue.append((child,depth+1)); return max_depth.

examples.trace: Step 1: Root1 not removed. BFS: start at (1,0). Step 2: Process node1 depth0, max_depth=0, children 2,3. Step 3: Process node2 depth1, but node2 is removed, skip. Step 4: Process node3 depth1, not removed, max_depth=1, children none. Queue empty. Max depth=1, height=1.

Step 1: Root1 not removed. BFS: (1,0). Step 2: Process node1 depth0, max_depth=0, child2. Step 3: Process node2 depth1, not removed, max_depth=1, child3. Step 4: Process node3 depth2, but removed, skip. Queue empty. Max depth=1, height=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Representation and Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Represent a tree using adjacency list and perform basic DFS/BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing a tree in code and traversing it. Intuition: trees are acyclic connected graphs, so we can use adjacency lists (list of lists) where each node has a list of its children. For rooted trees, edges are directed from parent to child. Traversal algorithms like DFS and BFS allow us to visit all nodes systematically. DFS goes deep first, using recursion or a stack, while BFS goes level by level using a queue. Algorithm to build adjacency list: given edges as pairs (parent, child), initialize adj list of size n+1, for each edge, append child to parent's list. DFS traversal: start at root, visit node, recursively visit each child. BFS traversal: use queue, start with root, while queue not empty, pop, visit, enqueue children. Time O(n) for building and traversal, space O(n) for adjacency list. This feeds into L2 by providing the tree structure needed for height calculation. Common mistakes: assuming 0-based vs 1-based indexing, not handling empty trees, or creating undirected adjacency (which works but may cause cycles in traversal). Real code pattern: n=5; adj=[[] for _ in range(n+1)]; for parent,child in edges: adj[parent].append(child).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj list size 5 (indices 0-4). Step 2: Edge (1,2): append 2 to adj[1]. Step 3: Edge (1,3): append 3 to adj[1]. Step 4: Edge (2,4): append 4 to adj[2]. Result: adj[0]=[], adj[1]=[2,3], adj[2]=[4], adj[3]=[], adj[4]=[].
Step 1: Initialize size 4. Step 2: Edge (1,2): adj[1]=[2]. Step 3: Edge (2,3): adj[2]=[3]. Result: adj[0]=[], adj[1]=[2], adj[2]=[3], adj[3]=[].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Tree
ladders.desc: Perform DFS traversal to visit all nodes in a tree recursively.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is DFS on a tree, which visits each node exactly once by exploring as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. Intuition: starting from the root, we visit a node, then recursively visit all its children, which naturally processes the entire tree. It's efficient and simple for trees due to no cycles. Algorithm: 1) Define function dfs(node): visit node (e.g., print or mark), then for each child in adjacency list, call dfs(child). 2) Base case: if node has no children, return after visiting. Time O(n) as each node visited once. Space O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. This feeds into L3 as a key traversal method. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if graph has cycles (not in trees), not handling disconnected nodes, or incorrect adjacency list. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, adj): print(node); for child in adj[node]: dfs(child, adj).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root1, visit 1. Step 2: Children of 1: 2 and 3. Step 3: Recurse to child2, visit 2, no children, return. Step 4: Recurse to child3, visit 3, no children, return. Done.

Step 1: Start at root1, visit 1. Step 2: Children of 1: 2. Step 3: Recurse to 2, visit 2. Step 4: Children of 2: 3 and 4. Step 5: Recurse to 3, visit 3, no children, return. Step 6: Recurse to 4, visit 4, no children, return. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree Structure and Node Relationships
ladders.desc: Understand basic tree terminology: root, parent, child, subtree, and height.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what a tree is and its properties. A tree is a hierarchical data structure with a root node and zero or more child nodes, where each child is the root of a subtree. Key terms: Root is the top node with no parent. Parent is a node with children. Child is a node directly connected below a parent. Subtree consists of a node and all its descendants. Height of a tree is the number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf (or sometimes nodes; here we use depth count from root). Intuition: trees model hierarchical relationships, like organization charts or file systems. This foundational knowledge feeds into L4 by providing the context for DFS traversal. Common mistakes: confusing height with depth (depth is distance from root to a node, height is max depth), or misunderstanding subtree inclusion (includes the node itself). Real code pattern: conceptual, no code yet, but think of a class Node with value and list of children.

examples.input: Tree: root A with children B and C, B has child D
examples.output: Root: A, Parent of D: B, Subtree at B: nodes B,D, Height: 2 (edges A-B-D)
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify root A. Step 2: Children of A: B and C. Step 3: Child of B: D. Step 4: Parent of D is B. Step 5: Subtree at B includes B and D. Step 6: Longest path from A to leaf: A-B-D has 2 edges, height=2.

examples.input: Tree: root X with child Y, Y has child Z
examples.output: Root: X, Parent of Z: Y, Subtree at Y: nodes Y,Z, Height: 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Root X. Step 2: Child of X: Y. Step 3: Child of Y: Z. Step 4: Parent of Z is Y. Step 5: Subtree at Y includes Y and Z. Step 6: Longest path X-Y-Z has 2 edges, height=2.

testCases.expected: Root: 1, Parent of 2: 1, Subtree at 2: nodes 2, Height: 1
Root: 1, Parent of 2: 1, Subtree at 2: nodes 2, Height: 1

testCases.input: Tree: root 1, child 2, child 2 has child 3
testCases.expected: Root: 1, Parent of 3: 2, Subtree at 2: nodes 2,3, Height: 2

testCases.input: Tree: root A, children B,C, B has child D, C has child E
testCases.expected: Root: A, Parent of D: B, Subtree at C: nodes C,E, Height: 2
Root: X, No parent, Subtree at X: nodes X, Height: 0

testCases.expected: Root: 1, Parent of 5: 4, Subtree at 2: nodes 2,4,5, Height: 3

testCases.input: Tree: root R, children A,B, A has child C, B has child D, C has child E
testCases.expected: Root: R, Parent of E: C, Subtree at A: nodes A,C,E, Height: 3

testCases.input: Tree: root M, child N, N has child O, O has child P
testCases.expected: Root: M, Parent of P: O, Subtree at N: nodes N,O,P, Height: 3
Root: 10, Parent of 20: 10, Subtree at 30: nodes 30, Height: 1

testCases.expected: Root: Alpha, Parent of Delta: Gamma, Subtree at Beta: nodes Beta,Gamma,Delta, Height: 3

approaches.name: Approach 2: Precomputed Heights with Lazy Removal
approaches.summary: Precompute the height of every node's subtree initially. When a subtree is removed, mark it as removed and update heights only for ancestors of the removed node by propagating height changes upward. This avoids full tree recomputation.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n + q * h) where h is tree height (worst case O(n))

ladders.title: Full Problem — Remove Subtree Queries with Height Updates
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of subtree removal queries with height recalculation using precomputed heights and lazy removal.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the problem of processing multiple subtree removal queries while efficiently maintaining the height of the remaining tree. The core idea is to precompute the height of every node's subtree during an initial DFS traversal. When a subtree rooted at node x is removed, we don't physically delete nodes but mark them as removed. The key optimization is that we only need to update heights for ancestors of the removed node, propagating height changes upward until we reach the root or encounter an ancestor whose height doesn't change. This avoids O(n) recomputation per query. The algorithm maintains three arrays: parent[] for parent relationships, height[] for subtree heights, and removed[] for removal status. For each query: 1) If node x is already removed, return current root height. 2) Mark x and all nodes in its subtree as removed (using DFS). 3) For each ancestor a of x (starting from parent[x] up to root): a) Recalculate height[a] as max(height[child]) + 1 over all non-removed children. b) If new height equals old height, stop propagation. 4) Return height[root]. The time complexity is O(n + q*h) where h is tree height (worst case O(n) for chain trees). Space is O(n). The intuition is that removing a subtree only affects heights of ancestors, not other parts of the tree, allowing localized updates.

examples.trace: Initial tree: 1(children:2,3), 2(children:4,5), others leaf. Precomputed heights: height[4]=1, height[5]=1, height[2]=2, height[3]=1, height[1]=3. Query 1: Remove subtree at 2. Mark 2,4,5 removed. Update ancestors: parent[2]=1. Recalculate height[1]: children are 2(removed),3(height=1). Max child height=1, so height[1]=2. Return 2. Query 2: Remove subtree at 3. Mark 3 removed. Update ancestors: parent[3]=1. Recalculate height[1]: children 2(removed),3(removed). No valid children, so height[1]=1. Return 1.

Initial heights: height[4]=1, height[3]=2, height[2]=1, height[1]=3. Query 1: Remove 4. Mark 4 removed. Update ancestors: parent[4]=3. Recalculate height[3]: children:4(removed). No valid children, height[3]=1. parent[3]=1. Recalculate height[1]: children:2(height=1),3(height=1). Max=1, height[1]=2? Wait, initial height[1]=3 based on path 1-3-4. After removing 4, height[3]=1, so height[1]=max(1,1)+1=2. Actually height[1] should be 3? Let's recalc: height[2]=1, height[3]=1 after update, so height[1]=max(1,1)+1=2. But tree has path 1-2 of length 2, so height=2. My mistake: initial height[1]=3 was wrong, should be 3? Path 1-3-4 gives depth 3, height=3. After removing 4, longest path is 1-2 of depth 2, height=2. So output 2. Query 2: Remove 3. Mark 3 removed (already removed 4). Update ancestors: parent[3]=1. Recalculate height[1]: children:2(height=1),3(removed). Max=1, height[1]=2. Return 2. Wait, after query 1, height was 2, after removing 3, tree has only node 1 and 2, height=2. So outputs: [2,2]. Let me correct initial: height[4]=1, height[3]=2, height[2]=1, height[1]=3. Query 1: Remove 4. Update height[3]=1 (since child 4 removed). Update height[1]=max(height[2]=1, height[3]=1)+1=2. Return 2. Query 2: Remove 3. Mark 3 removed. Update height[1]=max(height[2]=1)+1=2. Return 2. So output [2,2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Height Propagation After Subtree Removal
ladders.desc: Update heights of ancestors after removing a subtree, stopping when height doesn't change.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the efficient approach. After marking a subtree as removed, we need to update the heights of all ancestors of the removed node. The key insight is that height changes propagate upward but stop as soon as an ancestor's height remains unchanged, because if an ancestor's height doesn't change, its own ancestors won't be affected either. The algorithm: 1) Start from the parent of the removed node. 2) For each ancestor a in the path to root: a) Store old_height = height[a]. b) Compute new_height = 1 + max(height[child] for all children c of a where not removed[c]). If no valid children, new_height = 1 (node alone). c) If new_height == old_height, break (stop propagation). d) Else, update height[a] = new_height and move to parent[a]. 3) Continue until root or break condition. Time complexity per query is O(h) where h is tree height. Space is O(1) extra. The intuition is that removing a subtree can only decrease heights of ancestors, and the effect diminishes as we go up. This avoids unnecessary updates. Common mistakes: not stopping when height unchanged (causing O(n) updates), or incorrectly computing max over children. Real code pattern: while node != -1: old = height[node]; new = 1 + max((height[c] for c in children[node] if not removed[c]), default=0); if new == old: break; height[node] = new; node = parent[node].

examples.input: Tree: 1(children:2,3), heights: [1:3, 2:2, 3:1], removed subtree at 2, update ancestors
examples.trace: Start at parent of 2: node=1. old_height[1]=3. Children of 1: 2(removed), 3(height=1). Valid children: only 3. max_child_height=1. new_height=1+1=2. new != old, so update height[1]=2. Move to parent[1] (none). Stop. Final height[1]=2.

examples.input: Tree: 1(children:2),2(children:3), heights: [1:3,2:2,3:1], removed subtree at 3, update ancestors
examples.trace: Start at parent of 3: node=2. old_height[2]=2. Children of 2: 3(removed). No valid children. new_height=1 (default). new=1, old=2, update height[2]=1. Move to parent[2]=1. old_height[1]=3. Children of 1: 2(height=1). max_child_height=1. new_height=1+1=2. new=2, old=3, update height[1]=2. Move to parent[1] (none). Stop. Final heights: [1:2,2:1,3:1].

testCases.input: Node: 1, parent: -1, children: [], height: 1, removed: none
Node: 1, parent: -1, children: [2],2 children: [3], heights: [1:3,2:2,3:1], remove 3
Node: 1, parent: -1, children: [2,3],2 children: [4], heights: [1:3,2:2,3:1,4:1], remove 4
Node: 1, parent: -1, children: [2,3], heights: [1:2,2:1,3:1], remove 2 and 3 (sequential, test stop condition)
testCases.expected: after remove 2: height[1] remains 2; after remove 3: height[1] becomes 1

testCases.input: Node: 1, parent: -1, children: [2],2 children: [3],3 children: [4], heights: [1:4,2:3,3:2,4:1], remove 4
testCases.expected: height[3] becomes 1, height[2] becomes 2, height[1] becomes 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Precomputed Subtree Heights with Lazy Removal
ladders.desc: Maintain subtree heights and update only affected ancestors upon removal.
ladders.explanation: This level captures the central technique: instead of recomputing the entire tree height after each removal, we precompute the height of every node's subtree and update only the ancestors of removed nodes. The logic has two phases: 1) Initialization: Perform a DFS from the root to compute height[node] = 1 + max(height[child]) for all children, with base case height[leaf]=1. This takes O(n) time. 2) Query processing: For each removal of subtree at node x, mark x and all descendants as removed (using DFS or BFS). Then, for each ancestor a of x (from parent[x] up to root), recalculate height[a] based on its non-removed children. If height[a] doesn't change, stop propagation. The intuition is that removal only affects the heights of nodes on the path from x to root, and the effect diminishes upward. This reduces per-query time from O(n) to O(h). Space is O(n) for storing heights, parent links, and removal status. Common mistakes: forgetting to mark all descendants as removed, or not handling multiple removals correctly (a node can only be removed once). Real code pattern: heights = [0]*n; compute_heights_dfs(root); for query in queries: if removed[x]: continue; mark_removed_dfs(x); node = parent[x]; while node != -1: old = heights[node]; new = 1 + max(heights[c] for c in children[node] if not removed[c]); if new == old: break; heights[node] = new; node = parent[node]; answer.append(heights[root]).

examples.input: Tree with root 1, children 2 and 3. Precomputed heights: 1:3, 2:2, 3:1. Remove subtree at 2.
examples.trace: Initial: heights[1]=max(heights[2]=2, heights[3]=1)+1=3. Mark subtree at 2 as removed. Update ancestors: parent[2]=1. Recalc height[1]: children 2(removed),3(height=1). max=1, new height=2. Update height[1]=2. Stop. Final: height[1]=2.

examples.input: Chain tree: 1-2-3. Precomputed heights: 1:3,2:2,3:1. Remove subtree at 3.
examples.trace: Mark 3 removed. Update ancestors: parent[3]=2. Recalc height[2]: children:3(removed). No valid children, new height=1. Update height[2]=1. Move to parent[2]=1. Recalc height[1]: children:2(height=1). new height=2. Update height[1]=2. Stop.

testCases.expected: height[1] becomes 4? Wait, recalc: after remove 4, height[1]=max(3,2)+1=4, so remains 4

testCases.input: Tree: root 1, children 2 and 3, 2 has child 4, heights:1:3,2:2,3:1,4:1, remove 4
Tree: root 1, children 2 and 3, both leaves, heights:1:2,2:1,3:1, remove 2
Tree: root 1, child 2, 2 child 3, 3 child 4, heights:1:4,2:3,3:2,4:1, remove 4
testCases.expected: height[3] becomes 1, height[2] becomes 2, height[1] becomes 3

testCases.input: Tree: root 1, children 2,3,4 all leaves, heights:1:2,2:1,3:1,4:1, remove 2 and 3 (test multiple)
testCases.expected: after remove 2: height[1] remains 2; after remove 3: height[1] becomes 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Subtree Heights via DFS
ladders.desc: Calculate the height of every node's subtree using depth-first search.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing the height of each node's subtree in a tree. The height of a node is defined as the length of the longest path from that node to a leaf in its subtree. For a tree with n nodes, we can compute all subtree heights efficiently using a single DFS traversal. The algorithm: 1) Start DFS from the root. 2) For each node, recursively compute heights of all children first. 3) Then, height[node] = 1 + max(height[child] for all children), or 1 if the node is a leaf (no children). This works because the height of a node depends only on the heights of its children, so post-order traversal (children before parent) is ideal. Time complexity is O(n) since each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack in worst case (for a skewed tree). The intuition is bottom-up computation: leaves have height 1, and each parent's height is one more than the tallest child. This precomputation is crucial for the lazy removal approach, as it provides baseline heights that can be updated incrementally. Common mistakes: confusing height with depth (depth is distance from root, height is distance to deepest leaf), or not handling empty children correctly. Real code pattern: def compute_heights(node): if node is leaf: height[node]=1; return 1; max_child=0; for child in children[node]: max_child=max(max_child, compute_heights(child)); height[node]=1+max_child; return height[node].

examples.input: Tree: root 1 with children 2 and 3, both leaves
examples.trace: DFS: visit 2 (leaf), height[2]=1. Visit 3 (leaf), height[3]=1. Then at node 1, max_child_height = max(1,1)=1. height[1]=1+1=2.
DFS: visit 3 (leaf), height[3]=1. At node 2, max_child_height from child 3 is 1, height[2]=1+1=2. At node 1, max_child_height from child 2 is 2, height[1]=1+2=3.

testCases.input: Tree: root 1, children 2 and 3, 2 has child 4
Tree: balanced tree of depth 3 (7 nodes)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Traversal and Parent-Child Relationships
ladders.desc: Navigate a tree structure using DFS/BFS and maintain parent information.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing a tree and understanding parent-child relationships. A tree is a connected acyclic graph with n nodes and n-1 edges. For this problem, we typically represent the tree using adjacency lists or parent arrays. Key operations: 1) Building the tree: Given edges, create an adjacency list where each node has a list of its children. Often, we also store parent[node] for quick upward traversal. 2) Traversal: Depth-First Search (DFS) visits nodes recursively, exploring as deep as possible before backtracking. Breadth-First Search (BFS) visits nodes level by level. For height computation and subtree removal, DFS is natural because it processes children before parent (post-order). 3) Parent tracking: During traversal, we can record parent[node] to enable moving upward from a node to its ancestors. This is essential for the lazy removal approach to propagate height changes. Time complexity for building and traversing is O(n). Space is O(n) for adjacency lists. The intuition is that trees have hierarchical structure, and operations often involve moving between parents and children. Common mistakes: not handling bidirectional edges (treating them as undirected), or forgetting to skip parent during DFS to avoid cycles. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u); parent = [-1]*n; def dfs(node, par): parent[node]=par; for nei in adj[node]: if nei!=par: dfs(nei, node).

examples.trace: Start DFS at root 1 with parent -1. Visit neighbor 2: set parent[2]=1, recurse to 2. At 2, neighbors: [1] but 1 is parent, so skip. Back to 1, visit neighbor 3: set parent[3]=1, recurse to 3. At 3, neighbors: [1] skip. Done.
DFS at 1: parent[1]=-1. Visit 2: parent[2]=1. At 2, visit neighbor 3 (since 1 is parent, skip): parent[3]=2. At 3, neighbors: [2] skip. Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree Height and Subtree Removal
ladders.desc: Understand basic tree properties: height, subtree, and removal effect.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces key tree terminology and simple operations. A tree is a hierarchical structure with a root node and parent-child relationships. Key definitions: 1) Height of a tree: The length of the longest path from the root to a leaf. For a single node, height is 1 (or 0 if counting edges; here we use node count). 2) Subtree: For any node x, the subtree rooted at x consists of x and all its descendants. 3) Removing a subtree: When we remove the subtree at node x, we delete x and all nodes in its subtree from the tree. The remaining tree consists of all nodes not in that subtree. The effect on height: Removing a subtree can decrease the height of the tree if the removed subtree contained the longest path. Otherwise, height may remain unchanged. For example, in a tree with root 1 and children 2 and 3, if height is determined by path through 2, removing subtree at 3 doesn't change height. This basic understanding is prerequisite for the lazy removal approach, as we need to know which nodes' heights are affected. Common mistakes: confusing height with number of nodes, or not realizing that removal only affects ancestors. Real code pattern: Concept only, no code yet. Focus on examples: tree = {1: [2,3]}, height=2; remove subtree at 2 -> remaining tree has only node 1 and 3? Wait, if remove subtree at 2, nodes 2 and its descendants are gone, so remaining: 1 and 3, height=2 (path 1-3).

examples.input: Tree: root 1 with children 2 and 3 (all leaves). Height? Remove subtree at 2.
examples.output: Initial height: 2. After removal: tree has nodes 1 and 3, height=2.
examples.trace: Initial tree: nodes 1,2,3. Longest path: 1-2 or 1-3, length 2 edges? If height counts nodes, path has 2 nodes, height=2. Remove subtree at 2: remove node 2. Remaining: 1 and 3. Path 1-3 has 2 nodes, height=2.

examples.input: Chain tree: 1-2-3. Height? Remove subtree at 2.
examples.output: Initial height: 3. After removal: tree has node 1 only, height=1.
examples.trace: Initial: path 1-2-3 has 3 nodes, height=3. Remove subtree at 2: remove nodes 2 and 3. Remaining: node 1 alone, height=1.

testCases.input: Tree: single node 1. Remove subtree at 1. Remaining height?
testCases.expected: 1 (root alone, but technically tree empty? Assume root remains with height 1 if not removed)

testCases.input: Tree: 1-2. Remove subtree at 2. Remaining height?
Tree: 1 with children 2,3. Remove subtree at 3. Remaining height?
Tree: 1-2-3. Remove subtree at 3. Remaining height?
Tree: 1 with children 2,3,4. Remove subtree at 4. Remaining height?
